retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Active Tuberculosis Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The minimum requirement for the exclusion of active TB disease is the patient is asymptomatic of TB disease with a normal chest radiograph. [70] If there is uncertainty, obtaining three early morning sputum samples for acid-fast staining, mycobacterial culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing must be considered. Signs or symptoms of extrapulmonary TB disease may require imaging, biopsy, and lumbar puncture.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Chest Radiography and Sputum Samples
retrieved_docs.content: Another test for active TB is the evaluation of 3 early morning sputum samples for acid-fast staining, mycobacterial culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing. [22] Identifying M tuberculosis on a sputum sample is diagnostic for active TB infection; such a patient can infect others and requires isolation. This situation also requires public health notification for contact tracing to limit community transmission of TB. [50] Patients with isolated extrapulmonary manifestations of TB are noninfectious. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing latent TB infection can be challenging. No single test is adequate for diagnosis, and assessing for the condition requires careful clinical and radiological evaluation and interpretation of immunological tests. Patients with latent TB infection are asymptomatic by definition and detected solely through screening. The most pertinent differential diagnoses are active or previously treated TB disease and other NTM infections that predispose to falsely positive immune-based tests. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A chest x-ray is warranted for evaluation following a positive PPD/TST or NAAT test to determine if pulmonary involvement is present; in cases of high suspicion for M tuberculosis in the absence of a positive screening test, imaging may be performed to assess for silent intraparenchymal disease or adenopathy. Other studies depend upon the location of potential infectious foci and can include ultrasonography of enlarged lymph nodes, imaging of the central nervous system followed by lumbar puncture, skeletal x-rays, imaging of the gastrointestinal or genitourinary tract, or a combination of these tests. Unfortunately, the lack of a positive reaction to a tuberculin test does not always exclude TB infection or TB disease, nor does a positive reaction distinguish between latent TB and actual disease. Up to 40% of immunocompetent children with culture-positive TB disease did not initially react to the tuberculin test. Host factors, particularly young age (younger than 2 years), poor nutrition, immunosuppression, and concurrent viral infections, can adversely affect a positive result. Recent TB infection and overwhelming disease (eg, those seen in disseminated disease) may also result in a negative reaction. Immunosuppression, particularly in children with advanced HIV disease, can also lead to a false negative PPD/TST result.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Chest Radiography and Sputum Samples
retrieved_docs.content: A chest radiograph may be adequate to exclude active tuberculosis in patients without symptoms of TB disease. However, patients with clinical or radiological abnormalities should be further investigated for TB disease or other conditions. Other conditions that may mimic TB include silicosis, malignancy, sarcoidosis, autoimmune causes such as rheumatoid arthritis and vasculitis, and infections including NTM, Nocardia , Cryptococcus , Histoplasma, and Aspergillus. [45] [46] [47] [48] [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Resolved Tuberculosis Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic patients with immunological evidence of prior exposure to M tuberculosis characterized by  a positive TST or IGRA may have had a previously treated or cleared TB infection. Obtaining a careful timeframe of prior exposures and treatment history is critical. The decision to treat latent TB infection is often a patient-specific risk-benefit analysis; clinical calculators may assist the clinician. [62] [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: Interpretation criteria for a positive result of the TST can be found in the following table. [29] If a TST is positive in a situation of high to above baseline risk: The test should not be repeated. [13] A chest radiograph and clinical evaluation for the potential of active tuberculosis should be obtained. [13] If no evidence of active disease is noted by history, physical examination, or radiography, the patient should be deemed to have LTBI and given tuberculosis-preventative treatment. [13] If the chest radiograph or clinical evaluation suggests active tuberculosis, then active infection must be excluded with further testing. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with latent TB infection are asymptomatic. [19] Therefore, obtaining a medical history is dual-purposed: identifying patients with indications for testing for latent TB infection and excluding active tuberculosis disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Prevention, Control, and Elimination -- Clinical Significance -- Confirming TB Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Before diagnosing and treating a patient for TBI, clinicians must rule out TB disease in all those who screen positive to avert inadequate treatment of underlying TB disease. Ruling out TB disease requires a careful medical history and symptom review, targeted physical exam, and chest x-ray. A diagnosis of TBI is confirmed when a patient with a positive screening result has no symptoms of TB, and the chest x-ray and other diagnostic tests are negative. [7] [NSTC. LTBI ]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating the potential of active tuberculosis is imperative, as therapy for LTBI consists of 1 to 2 active agents, which is insufficient for treating active tuberculosis and can promote resistance. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: X-ray findings in most patients with primary TB show resolution of parenchymal lesions; occasionally, a Ranke complex is the only residual clue to a remote TB diagnosis. People with active TB disease may have a normal chest x-ray. Distinguishing active from inactive disease is always challenging when evaluating chest x-rays with pathologic changes. While clues such as cavity wall thickness, presence or absence of consolidation, fibrosis, pleural effusion, and calcifications may be present, no definitive findings can exclude active disease. CT images generally provide significantly improved sensitivity compared to x-rays for diagnosing TB. A few reports suggest that fludeoxyglucose F 18 (FDG) PET-CT may be useful for assessing response to therapy. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Tuberculosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No single test can accurately distinguish between active and latent TB disease. The diagnosis of latent TB infection relies on evidence of cellular immune response to M tuberculosis and the reasonable exclusion of active TB disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Chest x-rays and CT scans often reveal characteristic miliary patterns, showing widespread, small nodules throughout the lung fields. However, these imaging features may be absent in early and cryptic miliary TB.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications -- Test Selection
retrieved_docs.content: TST and IGRAs are used to diagnose LTBI and should not be used to exclude the possibility of active tuberculosis. [13] Both tests can be falsely negative in the setting of active disease. [28] Other testing modalities, such as chest imaging and sputum collection, should be employed if active tuberculosis is suspected. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Advantages and Disadvantages of TB Screening Tests in Low and Middle-Income Countries
retrieved_docs.content: Neither TSTs nor IGRAs can distinguish latent from active TB or predict who will evolve from latent infection to active disease. The clinician must exclude active TB when deciding to initiate therapy for latent TB; failure to do so will promote the emergence of resistant organisms and result in inadequate treatment outcomes. Individuals who have successfully eradicated their TB infection can continue to test positive by TST and IGRA; patients with a remote history of untreated latent TB can have a false negative test. [38] Excellent reviews explore detailed recommendations on latent TB testing strategies in different at-risk populations. [5] [34] A user-friendly, web-based, interactive program called the Online TST/IGRA Interpreter can estimate TB infection risk based on TST and IGRA results. [39] The algorithm incorporates individual parameters and calculates a positive predictive value.

retrieved_docs.content: Alternatively, a false positive test should be suspected in low-risk patients for prior exposure or disease progression. [13] For this reason, these patients should not be tested unless otherwise mandated. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: The finding described as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion” is classified as an “اضطراب منصفي” (segmental disorder). This indicates that the presence of fluid accumulation in the mediastinum identified on chest CT represents a localized, segmental pathological process. Recognizing mediastinal effusion on imaging as a segmental disorder helps clinicians diagnose and manage thoracic conditions that are confined to specific anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. Collectively, these relationships delineate an integrated framework in which mediastinal pathology—ranging from abscesses, segmental disorders, and various mediastinal masses (including calcium‑bearing lesions) to pleural‑mediastinal fistulas and miliary infiltrates—is situated within the category of other lower respiratory disease and is primarily evaluated through chest CT imaging. The hierarchical structure links general CT scanning to specialized chest CT procedures, to specific diagnostic findings, while positioning conventional chest x‑ray as a related but subordinate imaging option, thereby supporting comprehensive assessment and management of mediastinal and lower‑respiratory conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية are clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ and with تعاطي الكحول. Recognizing these associations aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients with suspected alcohol‑related lung disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: as masses of the mid and posterior mediastinum. It is also the primary imaging technique for identifying mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, encompassing unilateral right and left presentations, bilateral involvement, and combined mediastinal‑pleural fistula findings. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both القلق (anxiety) and with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray). Anxiety can amplify the perception of chest discomfort, while an abnormal chest X‑ray may reveal underlying pulmonary or cardiac pathology that also manifests as nonspecific chest pain. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may reflect both a psychological factor (القلق) and a radiographic abnormality (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) is essential for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and appropriate patient management.
